ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Chicken Vindaloo; closest defensible match is 'chicken curry' (FOODON_03315671). Vindaloo is a distinct Goan preparation but falls within the broader chicken curry category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Chicken dish) used as the closest defensible anchor. No specific Vindaloo code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Chicken Vindaloo or Goan chicken curry as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Chicken Vindaloo is approximately one bowl (350 g), typically accompanied by steamed rice or bread such as pav or naan.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Chicken Vindaloo are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using the closest available chicken curry / spiced chicken dish entries. Chicken Vindaloo is not listed as a standalone entry; values reflect a composite estimate for a Goan-style spiced chicken curry with vinegar-based marinade. Saturated fat and trans fat values were cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) chicken curry entries to supplement NIN data. Cholesterol value sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for chicken-based curry dishes.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Fiona S. Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Vindaloo in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous low-carbohydrate spiced chicken curry entries. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate (7.14 g/100g) from onions and spices; chicken and cooking fats contribute negligible glycemic impact. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as: (30 × 7.14 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 7.5 g, rounded to 1.5 per 100 g equivalent. GL per serving (350 g) ≈ 7.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (271 mg/100g; ~949 mg per 350 g serving) and potential for elevated fat from cooking oil
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g per meal, request reduced salt preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or papadums.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (7.14 g/100g) and low estimated glycemic index (~30); high protein supports satiety and glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Vindaloo is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when prepared without added sugar; pair with a small portion of basmati rice or cauliflower rice and monitor total carbohydrate intake from accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.29 g/100g) and elevated sodium; trans fat present at 0.14 g/100g from cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose preparations using minimal oil, avoid ghee or palm oil, and limit serving size to 150–200 g to keep saturated fat and sodium within daily cardiovascular targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory compounds from ginger, garlic, and capsaicin in chilies may provide modest analgesic benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Most individuals with rheumatoid arthritis can consume Chicken Vindaloo in moderation; those who find nightshade vegetables (chilies) exacerbate joint symptoms should reduce chili content or avoid the dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Vindaloo is a fiery, tangy Goan curry with deep Portuguese culinary roots, derived from the dish 'carne de vinha d'alhos' (meat marinated in wine and garlic). In the Goan adaptation, wine was replaced with local palm vinegar and the heat was dramatically amplified with dried Kashmiri red chilies, producing the dish's characteristic vivid red colour and sharp, sour-spicy flavour. Chicken pieces are marinated for several hours in a pungent paste of palm vinegar, garlic, ginger, cumin, cinnamon, cloves, and black pepper, then slow-simmered with caramelised onions until the sauce thickens and the chicken is tender. The texture is saucy with a slightly oily surface; the flavour is intensely spiced, sour from the vinegar, and deeply savoury. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 157 kcal, 12.9 g protein, 8.6 g fat, and only 7.1 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 30. Sodium is elevated at 271 mg/100g, which is a concern for hypertension management. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal when prepared with permissible ingredients. It is a staple of Goan restaurant menus and is widely served across India and in Indian diaspora communities globally. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low carbohydrate load, but sodium content warrants caution for hypertensive individuals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken vindaloo?
Is chicken vindaloo good for diabetics?
What is the difference between vindaloo and other curries?
What spices are in chicken vindaloo?

confidence_reason: Chicken Vindaloo is not listed as a standalone entry in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo databases; nutrient values are adapted from the closest available chicken curry and spiced chicken dish entries in ICMR-NIN. Cholesterol value sourced from HPB. Saturated fat and trans fat cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Goan-style chicken vindaloo is not available as a standalone entry in ICMR-NIN, HPB Singapore Foodo, or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in the consulted approved sources. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 per the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for chicken-based curry; this is noted as potentially underreported for a dish containing chicken with skin in some preparations — confirmed zero per HPB source consulted. 4. trans_fat_g of 0.14 g/100g is a low but non-zero value derived from cooking oil used in preparation; this is plausible for a dish cooked with vegetable oil. 5. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database low-carbohydrate spiced chicken curry entries; no direct GI measurement for Chicken Vindaloo was found. Glycemic load per 350 g serving ≈ 7.5 g. 6. Portions in the input contained four duplicate entries for '1 serving (250g)'; these have been deduplicated to a single entry alongside the 350 g bowl serving. 7. The input alias entry labelled language 'ko' (Korean) containing the Devanagari string 'चिकन विंदाळू' appears to be a data error (Marathi/Hindi script assigned to Korean language code); this has been corrected — the Hindi alias 'चिकन विंदालू' is retained and the erroneous Korean-labelled Devanagari entry has been removed. No Korean-script alias exists for this dish.
